reference: GATE CS — Compiler Design / Frontend Phases

map.condition: Attribute computed from parent or siblings' attributes

map.use: \text{Bottom-up (LR) parser evaluates in a single pass}
\text{Top-down (LL) or SDT with actions in rule bodies}

map.condition: Evaluating an arithmetic expression without function calls

formulas.name: Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD)
formulas.latex: \text{SDD} = \text{CFG} + \{\text{semantic rules attaching attributes to grammar symbols}\}
formulas.plain: SDD = CFG + semantic rules attaching attributes to grammar symbols
formulas.whenToUse: When formalizing the association between grammar productions and attribute computations.

terms.means: Context-Free Grammar (productions)
equations that define attribute values

formulas.trap: An SDD is a specification, not a translation scheme; it does not specify evaluation order.

formulas.name: Syntax-Directed Translation Scheme (SDTS)
formulas.latex: \text{SDTS}: \text{CFG production with embedded semantic actions (program fragments)}
formulas.plain: SDTS: CFG production with embedded semantic actions (program fragments)
formulas.whenToUse: When semantic actions are placed at specific positions within grammar rule bodies to control execution order.

terms.means: code fragment executed when reached during parsing

formulas.trap: In an LR parser, actions embedded mid-rule may need marker non-terminals; actions at rule end are executed on reduce.

formulas.name: Synthesized Attribute
formulas.latex: \text{For } A \to X_1 X_2 \cdots X_n:\quad A.a = f(X_1.a_1,\, X_2.a_2,\, \ldots,\, X_n.a_n)
formulas.plain: A.a = f(X1.a1, X2.a2, ..., Xn.an)  [defined from children or terminals]
formulas.whenToUse: When an attribute of a non-terminal is computed from attributes of its children in the parse tree.

terms.means: parent non-terminal
grammar symbol (child) in the production body
the attribute being defined

formulas.trap: The start symbol has no parent, so all its attributes must be synthesized (or no inherited attrs used at root).

formulas.name: Inherited Attribute
formulas.latex: \text{For } A \to X_1 X_2 \cdots X_n:\quad X_j.b = g(A.a,\, X_1.c_1,\, \ldots,\, X_{j-1}.c_{j-1})
formulas.plain: Xj.b = g(A.a, X1.c1, ..., X_{j-1}.c_{j-1})  [from parent or left siblings]
formulas.whenToUse: When passing context (type, scope, etc.) downward or left-to-right through the parse tree.

terms.means: attribute of the parent non-terminal
inherited attribute of the j-th child

formulas.trap: A terminal's attribute value comes from the lexer; terminals cannot have inherited attributes defined by semantic rules.

formulas.name: S-Attributed SDD
formulas.latex: \text{S-attributed:}\quad \text{only synthesized attributes}\;\Rightarrow\;\text{evaluate bottom-up (LR parse)}
formulas.plain: S-attributed: only synthesized attributes => evaluate bottom-up (LR parse)
formulas.whenToUse: To determine if a single bottom-up pass is sufficient to evaluate all attributes.

terms.means: no attribute depends on parent/sibling values

formulas.trap: S-attributed does NOT mean the grammar itself is S-type; it is a property of the SDD, not the grammar class.

formulas.name: L-Attributed SDD
formulas.latex: \text{L-attributed:}\quad X_j.b = g\!\left(A.a,\, X_1.c_1,\, \ldots,\, X_{j-1}.c_{j-1}\right) \;\forall j
formulas.plain: L-attributed: each inherited attribute of Xj depends only on A's attrs and left siblings X1..X_{j-1}
formulas.whenToUse: Checking if one left-to-right depth-first traversal (or LL parse) can evaluate the SDD.

terms.means: position of symbol in production body (1-indexed)
inherited attribute of the head non-terminal

formulas.trap: Every S-attributed SDD is also L-attributed, but not vice versa.

formulas.name: Dependency Graph & Evaluation Order
formulas.latex: \text{Evaluation order} = \text{topological sort of attribute dependency graph}
formulas.plain: Evaluation order = topological sort of attribute dependency graph
formulas.whenToUse: When finding a valid order to evaluate attributes in a given parse tree.

terms.means: directed graph where edge A.a -> B.b means B.b is needed to compute A.a

formulas.trap: If the dependency graph has a cycle, the SDD is not well-defined (circular attributes).

formulas.name: LR Parser: Action Timing (Postfix SDTS)
formulas.latex: A \to X_1 X_2 \cdots X_n\;\{\text{action}\} \Rightarrow \text{action executes at REDUCE step}
formulas.plain: In LR parser: action placed at end of production body executes when the parser reduces by that rule
formulas.whenToUse: When tracing what an LR (shift-reduce) parser prints or computes for a given SDTS.

terms.means: parser replaces RHS symbols on stack with LHS non-terminal
semantic action (e.g., print, push, compute)

formulas.trap: Actions are executed in reduce order (bottom-up), so for E->E+T {print '+'}, '+' prints after the operands, producing postfix output.

formulas.name: Postfix SDT Output Order
formulas.latex: E \to E_1 \oplus E_2\;\{\text{print }\oplus\} \Rightarrow \text{operands printed first, operator last}
formulas.plain: Production E -> E op E { print op } with LR parser yields postfix (RPN) output
formulas.whenToUse: When computing the output string produced by an SDTS on an LR parser for arithmetic expressions.

terms.means: binary operator (+, -, *, .)
Reverse Polish Notation / postfix

formulas.trap: Students confuse infix vs postfix: { print op } at end of rule always gives postfix, not infix.

formulas.name: Marker Non-Terminal for Mid-Rule Actions
formulas.latex: A \to B\;\{\text{act}\}\;C \equiv A \to B\,M\,C,\quad M \to \varepsilon\;\{\text{act}\}
formulas.plain: Mid-rule action { act } is converted to a marker non-terminal M -> epsilon { act }
formulas.whenToUse: When converting an SDTS with mid-rule actions to a form compatible with an LR parser.

terms.means: fresh marker (epsilon) non-terminal
empty string

formulas.trap: Adding marker non-terminals may introduce reduce-reduce conflicts in the LR parser.

formulas.name: Annotated Parse Tree
formulas.latex: \text{Annotated parse tree} = \text{parse tree} + \text{attribute values at each node}
formulas.plain: Annotated parse tree = parse tree with attribute values written at every node
formulas.whenToUse: When manually evaluating an SDD by constructing and annotating the parse tree.

terms.means: concrete value of the attribute for that particular tree node

formulas.trap: Inherited attributes flow top-down; drawing arrows upward for inherited values is incorrect.

formulas.name: Type Propagation via Inherited Attributes
formulas.latex: D \to T\;L,\quad L.\text{inh} = T.\text{type},\quad L \to L_1\,\mathbf{,}\,\text{id}\;\{L_1.\text{inh}=L.\text{inh};\;\text{addtype}(\text{id},L.\text{inh})\}
formulas.plain: D -> T L, L.inh = T.type; L -> L1 , id { L1.inh = L.inh; addtype(id, L.inh) }
formulas.whenToUse: When writing semantic rules to attach types to identifier entries during a declaration.

terms.means: inherited type attribute flowing down the list
procedure to update symbol-table entry with type
synthesized type from the type specifier

formulas.trap: If you use synthesized attributes for type propagation across a list without rewriting the grammar, L-attributed rules with inherited attrs are needed.

formulas.name: Symbol Table Update in SDT
formulas.latex: \text{addtype}(\text{id}.\text{lexval},\;T.\text{type}) \Rightarrow \text{inserts type into symbol table at semantic action time}
formulas.plain: addtype(id.lexval, T.type) inserts type for identifier into the symbol table
formulas.whenToUse: When tracing which identifiers get what types added to the symbol table during semantic analysis.

terms.means: lexeme / name of the identifier from the lexer
type (int, float, pointer, etc.)

formulas.trap: Identifiers are added when their declarations are processed, not when they are used.

formulas.name: Stack Requirement for Expression Evaluation
formulas.latex: \text{Evaluating arithmetic expression (no function calls):}\quad \text{ONE stack suffices}
formulas.plain: Evaluating an arithmetic expression without embedded function calls: one stack is enough
formulas.whenToUse: When asked how many stacks are needed to evaluate an expression without function calls.

terms.means: LIFO data structure used to hold operands/operators

formulas.trap: With function calls, additional stacks (or an activation record stack) may be needed. Without function calls, one stack is always sufficient.

formulas.name: Expression Tree Evaluation
formulas.latex: \text{Value}(\text{node}) = \begin{cases} \text{leaf value} & \text{if leaf} \\ \text{op}\left(\text{Value}(\text{left}),\, \text{Value}(\text{right})\right) & \text{if internal node} \end{cases}
formulas.plain: Value(node) = leaf_value if leaf; else op(Value(left), Value(right))
formulas.whenToUse: When computing the value of an expression represented as a binary expression tree.

terms.means: operator at the internal node (+, -, *, /)
numeric literal or variable value (0 or 1 in typical GATE problems)

formulas.trap: To maximize/minimize the expression value over all leaf assignments, enumerate systematically rather than greedily.

formulas.name: Infix, Postfix, Prefix from Expression Tree
formulas.latex: \text{Inorder} \Rightarrow \text{Infix},\quad \text{Postorder} \Rightarrow \text{Postfix (RPN)},\quad \text{Preorder} \Rightarrow \text{Prefix}
formulas.plain: Inorder traversal -> infix; Postorder -> postfix (RPN); Preorder -> prefix
formulas.whenToUse: When deriving the output of an SDTS or expression tree traversal.

terms.means: left, root, right
left, right, root
root, left, right

formulas.trap: LR-parser SDTS with { print op } at rule end always produces postfix, matching postorder traversal.

formulas.name: LL vs LR Suitability for SDT
formulas.latex: \text{S-attributed} \Rightarrow \text{LR parse}\;(\text{bottom-up}),\quad \text{L-attributed} \Rightarrow \text{LL parse}\;(\text{top-down})
formulas.plain: S-attributed SDD -> LR (bottom-up) parser; L-attributed SDD -> LL (top-down) parser
formulas.whenToUse: When choosing or justifying which parser type can evaluate a given SDD in one pass.

terms.means: all attributes synthesized
synthesized + left-to-right inherited attributes

formulas.trap: L-attributed SDDs can also be evaluated with LR parsers using extra mechanisms (e.g., extra stack for inherited attrs), but LL is the natural fit.

formulas.name: Semantic Rule (SDD) vs Semantic Action (SDTS)
formulas.latex: \text{Semantic rule (SDD): declarative equation}\quad\text{vs}\quad\text{Semantic action (SDTS): imperative code}
formulas.plain: SDD semantic rule: declarative (equation defining an attribute); SDTS semantic action: imperative (executable code)
formulas.whenToUse: When distinguishing between SDD notation and SDTS notation in GATE questions.

terms.means: defines WHAT value is, not HOW/WHEN to compute it
specifies executable program fragment with explicit ordering

formulas.trap: Mixing up SDD (specification) with SDTS (implementation) can lead to wrong answers about evaluation order.

formulas.name: Attribute Values at Terminal Nodes
formulas.latex: \text{Terminal } t:\quad t.\text{lexval} = \text{value supplied by lexer (token attribute)}
formulas.plain: For terminal t: t.lexval is provided by the lexer, not computed by semantic rules
formulas.whenToUse: When tracing how identifier names or literal values enter the attribute computation.

terms.means: lexical value (e.g., identifier name, integer constant)

formulas.trap: Do not write semantic rules that define a terminal's attribute; it is always supplied by the lexer.

formulas.name: Inherited Attributes in LR Parsers
formulas.latex: \text{Inherited attributes cannot be computed directly on the LR parse stack without extra mechanisms}
formulas.plain: Inherited attributes are not naturally computed by an LR parser; workarounds include extra stack or grammar transformation
formulas.whenToUse: When asked why an SDD with inherited attributes cannot be directly implemented with a shift-reduce parser.

terms.means: stack holding states and grammar symbols

formulas.trap: Some inherited attributes CAN be simulated on an LR stack if the grammar allows copying them as synthesized attributes of a sub-non-terminal.
